content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 27, 2009


Albert C. Adam
Senior Vice President – Investments
Wachovia Securities
Adam-Remmenga Investment Group
2829 Townsgate Road, Suite 200
Westlake Village, California 91361

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-09-068

Dear Mr. Adam:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Also, please note that our advice is
based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the
application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest. This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that may have
already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1.    If, as a member of a city planning commission you were prohibited under Section
84308 from participating in a planning commission matter, would Section 84308 also
prohibit you from speaking to the city council on the same matter as a member of the
general public during the public comment section of a city council meeting?

2.    Does Section 84308 or any other provision of the Act prohibit you from speaking
about the same issue to neighborhood groups?

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-068
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1.      No. Section 84308 does not prohibit you from speaking on the issue as a member
of the general public during the public comment section at the next city council meeting
because the matter is no longer before your agency.

2.      No. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from speaking about this issue to
neighborhood groups.

FACTS

        You are a member of the City of Thousand Oaks Planning Commission. Home
Depot is seeking to build a new store at the site of a vacant Kmart. You recused yourself
from a Planning Commission decision where Home Depot was the applicant because you
had received campaign contributions in excess of $250 from parties opposed to the
development of a Home Depot in Thousand Oaks. The Planning Commission approved
the Home Depot application. This approval has now been appealed to the Thousand
Oaks City Council. You would like to speak to the city council about this appeal during
the public comment section of the city council meeting taking place on March 31, 2009,
as a private citizen. You would also like to speak to some neighborhood groups about the
issue.

ANALYSIS

        Section 84308 addresses the issue of when a campaign contribution may result in
an obligation for a public official to disqualify from participating in a governmental
decision. Subdivision (c) states the following:

        "Prior to rendering any decision in a proceeding involving a
license, permit or other entitlement for use pending before any
agency, each officer of the agency who received a contribution
within the preceding 12 months in an amount of more than two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) from a party or from any participant
shall disclose that fact on the record of the proceeding. No officer
of an agency shall make, participate in making or in any way
attempt to use his or her official position to influence the decision
in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for
use pending before the agency if the officer has willfully or
knowingly received a contribution in an amount of more than two
hundred fifty ($250) within the preceding 12 months from a party
or his or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent if
the officer knows or has reason to know that the participant has a
financial interest in the decision, as that term is described with

File No. A-09-068
Page No. 3

respect to public officials in Article 1 (commencing with Section
87100) of Chapter 7."

A "participant" is any person who is not a party but who actively supports
or opposes a particular decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or
other entitlement for use and who has a financial interest in the decision. (Section
84308(a)(2).) According to your facts, you received more than $250 from a
person who may have been a participant in the proceeding.²

Section 84308 applies only to matters that are before a public official's own
agency. (See Regulation 18438.1(a).) Although the Commission has not specifically
defined the phrase "make, participate in making, or in any way attempting to use his or
in the Act's basic conflict-of-interest provision in Section 87100 and defined in detail in
Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.4. Regulation 18702.3(a) states that an official is
attempting to influence his or her own agency on when the governmental decision in
question is "within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed or subject to the
budgetary control of his or her own agency." On this basis, the Commission has
generally treated different city departments as different agencies. (Stout Advice Letter,
No. I-88-313, copy enclosed.) You have indicated that the Thousand Oaks City
Council is subject to the budgetary control of the Planning Commission in any way.

Therefore, you are not prohibited from speaking during the public comment
section of the next city council meeting on the appeal of the Home Depot decision as a
member of the general public. Under the same analogy to the Act's conflict-of-interest
regulations made above, you are also not prohibited from speaking to neighborhood
groups about this issue in your capacity as a private citizen.
(See Regulation 18202.4 (b)(2).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[illegible]
By: Sukhi K. Brar
SKB:jgl     Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure

² You have not indicated if the groups from which you received the contributions of $250 or more
have one or more financial interests in the decision. If not, they were not "participants" in the proceeding
and you were not prohibited from voting.

sections.question:
1. If, as a member of a city planning commission you were prohibited under Section 84308 from participating in a planning commission matter, would Section 84308 also prohibit you from speaking to the city council on the same matter as a member of the general public during the public comment section of a city council meeting?

2. Does Section 84308 or any other provision of the Act prohibit you from speaking about the same issue to neighborhood groups?
sections.conclusion:
1. No. Section 84308 does not prohibit you from speaking on the issue as a member of the general public during the public comment section at the next city council meeting because the matter is no longer before your agency.

2. No. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from speaking about this issue to neighborhood groups.
sections.facts: You are a member of the City of Thousand Oaks Planning Commission. Home Depot is seeking to build a new store at the site of a vacant Kmart. You recused yourself from a Planning Commission decision where Home Depot was the applicant because you had received campaign contributions in excess of $250 from parties opposed to the development of a Home Depot in Thousand Oaks. The Planning Commission approved the Home Depot application. This approval has now been appealed to the Thousand Oaks City Council. You would like to speak to the city council about this appeal during the public comment section of the city council meeting taking place on March 31, 2009, as a private citizen. You would also like to speak to some neighborhood groups about the issue.
sections.analysis:
Section 84308 addresses the issue of when a campaign contribution may result in an obligation for a public official to disqualify from participating in a governmental decision. Subdivision (c) states the following:

"Prior to rendering any decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use pending before any agency, each officer of the agency who received a contribution within the preceding 12 months in an amount of more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250) from a party or from any participant shall disclose that fact on the record of the proceeding. No officer of an agency shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his or her official position to influence the decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use pending before the agency if the officer has willfully or knowingly received a contribution in an amount of more than two hundred fifty ($250) within the preceding 12 months from a party or his or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent if the officer knows or has reason to know that the participant has a financial interest in the decision, as that term is described with

respect to public officials in Article 1 (commencing with Section 87100) of Chapter 7."

A "participant" is any person who is not a party but who actively supports or opposes a particular decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use and who has a financial interest in the decision. (Section 84308(a)(2).) According to your facts, you received more than $250 from a person who may have been a participant in the proceeding.2

Section 84308 applies only to matters that are before a public official's own agency. (See Regulation 18438.1(a).) Although the Commission has not specifically defined the phrase "make, participate in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence" for purposes of Section 84308, that phrase is mirrored in the Act's basic conflict-of-interest provision in Section 87100 and defined in detail in Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.4. Regulation 18702.3(a) states that an official is attempting to influence his or her own agency only when the governmental decision in question is "within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed or subject to the budgetary control of his or her own agency." On this basis, the Commission has generally treated different city departments as different agencies. (Stout Advice Letter, No. I-88-313, copy enclosed.) You have not indicated that the Thousand Oaks City Council is subject to the budgetary control of the Planning Commission in any way.

Therefore, you are not prohibited from speaking during the public comment section of the next city council meeting on the appeal of the Home Depot decision as a member of the general public. Under the same analogy to the Act's conflict-of-interest regulations made above, you are also not prohibited from speaking to neighborhood groups about this issue in your capacity as a private citizen. (See Regulation 18202.4 (b)(2).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. If, as a member of a city planning commission you were prohibited under Section 84308 from participating in a planning commission matter, would Section 84308 also prohibit you from speaking to the city council on the same matter as a member of the general public during the public comment section of a city council meeting?

2. Does Section 84308 or any other provision of the Act prohibit you from speaking about the same issue to neighborhood groups?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Section 84308 does not prohibit you from speaking on the issue as a member of the general public during the public comment section at the next city council meeting because the matter is no longer before your agency.

2. No. Nothing in the Act prohibits you from speaking about this issue to neighborhood groups.
